query: undifferentiated mesenchymal cells in dental pulp and their potency

retrieved_docs.title: Regenerative Therapy in Pain -- Introduction -- Mesenchymal Stem Cell
retrieved_docs.content: According to the International Society for Cellular Therapy, the criteria for cells to be considered mesenchymal stem cells include: Plastic adherence when maintained under standard culture Expression of CD73, CD 90, and CD 105 and not the expression of CD11b, CD14, CD19, CD34, CD45, or HLA-DR Ability to differentiate into osteoblasts, chondroblasts, and adipocytes in vitro [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Structure and Function -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The defensive role of the pulp occurs through the development of new dentin, which can provide a barrier between irritants and slow the rate of carious decay. This process is mediated by the pulp, which spurs odontoblasts into action or initiates the production of new ones to form this needed hard tissue. The type and amount of dentin produced depend on numerous factors, including the source of damage (pathogen, thermal, chemical), the depth of damage, its severity, and the amount of surface area involved. The fact odontoblasts underline the significance of the pulp in dentin formation do not participate in cell division. Consequently, the formation of additional dentin relies on the pool of undifferentiated mesenchymal populations in the cell-rich zone to replace them. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Introduction -- Histocyte Derived
retrieved_docs.content: Benign Central giant cell granulomas Malignant Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Neck Movements -- Embryology -- The Role of Mesenchymal Stem Cells in the Development of the Skeletal System
retrieved_docs.content: Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) are multipotent in that they can form a variety of progenitor cells and then differentiate into cells belonging to the bone, cartilage, muscle, and fat cell lineages. Skeletal stem cells reside in multiple sites, including the perichondrium of fetal bone, the perichondrium of adult bone, and bone marrow. [7] MSCs are currently used to repair articular cartilage and intervertebral discs. [8] [9] Another critical characteristic of MSCs is that they can form new MSCs, thus maintaining the pool of stem cells belonging to the mesenchymal lineage. The current concept is that multiple types of stem and progenitor cells cooperate in forming, developing, and maintaining cells belonging to the osteogenic and other lineages associated with the vertebral column. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: There are two main regions of pulpal architecture: the central and the peripheral. On the periphery of the pulp, the area adjacent to the calcified dentin exists a series of different structural layers. At the interface of the dentin-pulp complex exists a layer of columnar odontoblast cells, whose primary function is the development of dentin. Here, many dentinal tubules, channels created by the odontoblasts, extend through the dentin from the pulp to the enamel border. [3] Each one of these tubules contains an odontoblast process, a cellular extension of an odontoblast used to form the dentin, as well as dentinal fluid. Like enamel, dentin is avascular, and these tubules are crucial for providing an entryway for nutrients in the interstitial fluid that originated from capillaries in the pulp. Beneath the odontoblastic layer is the cell-free zone, or zone of Weil, an area rich in both capillaries and nerve networks. The peripheral compartment closest to the central zone is the cell-rich zone, a layer rich in fibroblasts and undifferentiated mesenchymal cell that functions in supporting the population of odontoblasts by proliferation and differentiation. This cell layer can also differentiate into fibroblasts and macrophages. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Issues of Concern -- Biocompatibility
retrieved_docs.content: Ensuring the biocompatibility of Biodentine is crucial, especially when it comes into direct contact with periradicular and pulpal cells. Overall, research supports Biodentine's biocompatibility, with favorable results regarding cell adhesion and growth compared to glass ionomer cement. Studies utilizing three-dimensional multicellular spheroid cultures and human dental pulp stem cells confirmed the biocompatible and bioactive characteristics of Biodentine. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Recent studies show that the possibility to regenerate the dental pulp exists, through transplantation of dental pulp stem cells with pulpitis, on a human model.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The development of the pulp occurs in three stages: the bud, cap, and bell stage of development. In the bud stage, epithelial cells of the dental lamina, a band of epithelial cells protruding from the oral epithelium, proliferate and produce a bud-like projection into adjacent ectomesenchyme. This rounded epithelial bud gradually enlarges and forms a concave surface, marking the cap stage. At this point, the oral epithelial cells and mesenchymal cells have now become the enamel organ and dental papilla, respectively. Lastly, the bell stage takes place, in which morphodifferentiation and histodifferentiation of the pulp occur. Here, cells in the periphery differentiate into odontoblasts, secrete predentin, a collagenous matrix that mineralizes to become dentin, as they develop their columnar shape. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The pulp can be anatomically divided into four zones: the odontoblastic layer, mesenchymal zone, cell-rich zone, and the core. The odontoblasts are aligned in the periphery of the pulp and produce secondary or tertiary dentin; the second layer is mostly acellular and has loose connective tissue with nerve fibers and small blood vessels; the adjacent area is more cellular and has a rich vascular and nerve supply, similar to the central core located in the center of the pulp chamber.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Osteoprogenitor Cells -- Histochemistry and Cytochemistry
retrieved_docs.content: Several research groups have studied the development of osteoprogenitor cells. A study suggests that the perichondrial Thy-1–positive cells demonstrate potential osteogenic activity and participate in osteoblast formation during endochondral ossification. Another study shows intense alkaline phosphatase activity 2 weeks after osteogenic induction and the presence of mineralized nodules. The expression of bone sialoprotein, dentin matrix protein-1, and osteocalcin increases due to ALP proliferation. As confirmed by flow cytometry, 55 to 65% of cells display the cell-surface markers Sca1+ and Thy1+ in vitro expansion for an alpha-SMA-GFP positive population. The alpha-SMA–GFP-positive population also exhibits higher proliferative and osteogenic probabilities compared to an alpha-SMA–GFP-negative population. [8] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The dental papilla is a fusion of ectomesenchymal cells, termed odontoblasts, which form dentin, the yellow substance that forms the bulk of the teeth. Dentin consists of calcium, phosphorus, and hydroxyapatite crystals bound together in a hard crystalline substance. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The condensed mesenchymal cells beneath the inner enamel epithelium form the dental papilla, which later gives forms pulp. The enamel organ has a surrounding fibrous capsule known as the dental follicle, which later forms the periodontal ligament.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: To understand dental pulp development, it is best to view it through the lens of general tooth development. The initiation and progression of the tooth, and therefore pulpal, development is dependent on bidirectional cell signaling between the oral epithelium and surrounding mesenchyme. Teeth develop from two types of cells, oral epithelial cells and mesenchymal cells, which eventually form the enamel organ and dental papilla, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cells that define the disease known as سرطانة العظم (bone cancer).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are abnormal cells that characterize cancers classified as other and unspecified primary, indicating that these atypical cells constitute the pathological basis of such unspecified primary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells represent the abnormal cellular component that defines and drives the disease known as السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that the presence of these atypical, cancerous cells is the hallmark of this specific malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding that indicates the presence of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue samples signals that cancerous cells have invaded the bone, serving as an important diagnostic marker for confirming bone malignancy and guiding subsequent therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2716
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are identified as the abnormal cells that underlie the disease entity known as bone tumor (ورم العظام).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.
